title: Coalition vow to cut off welfare to non-citizens
full_text:
New migrants will be barred from accessing welfare handouts until they become Australian citizens, under an Opposition plan to fight back against runaway migration.
Opposition Leader Angus Taylor will make the landmark policy announcement tonight in his budget reply speech, in which he will say such taxpayer-funded support should be limited to “those who have built and contributed to our country”.
Currently, non-citizens are eligible for at least 17 different welfare and benefit programs ranging from the Age Pension and Disability Support Pension to JobSeeker and Youth Allowance as well as the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS).
Under the Opposition’s proposed policy, would-be welfare claimants would have to become citizens to access the support, a process that takes a minimum of four years.
Countries such as Sweden have increased this threshold to eight years recently because of concerns over migrant integration and welfare dependency.
The announcement comes on the back of Treasurer Jim Chalmers’ big-taxing budget handed down on Tuesday, which revealed the Labor government expects to increase spending by more than $41bn in the coming fiscal year.
It is understood the Coalition believes limiting welfare payments to Australian citizens would save the budget billions of dollars. Within the NDIS alone, enrolment data suggests that between July 2021 and December 2024 more than 65,000 non-citizens were signed up to the scheme, representing more than 8 per cent of the total number of participants.
“Australia is one of the most generous countries in the world, and we are proud of that,” Mr Taylor said. “But our welfare system, the NDIS and taxpayer-funded support should be there first and foremost for Australian citizens.
“Right now, some welfare payments can be accessed with little or no waiting period. Australians think that’s unfair.
“Our social safety net should support the people who have built and contributed to this country.” Mr Taylor will also criticise the Albanese government for handing out welfare to non-citizens while cutting the private health insurance rebate for over-65s.
The announcement comes after the Coalition declared it would also restrict eligibility for the Albanese government’s 5 per cent first home buyers’ deposit scheme, after it was revealed about 16 per cent of those accessing the program were non-citizens.
Under the Coalition’s plans, humanitarian entrants would still have access to taxpayer-funded support, while emergency assistance programs and domestic violence and child protection services would remain accessible to non-citizens.
Although the Coalition has not nominated a target figure for net overseas migration, Mr Taylor is also expected to underline the need to link population intake to housing in his speech tonight, while proposing a suite of cuts to red tape to lift Australia’s home construction rates.
The Albanese government has seen more than a million new migrants arrive since taking office in 2022.
summary: The Coalition plans to restrict welfare access for new migrants until they become Australian citizens, aiming to save billions and address concerns over migrant integration.
author: James Morrow
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: Non-citizens currently access at least 17 welfare programs, including the NDIS.
The proposed policy would require a minimum four-year wait for welfare eligibility after obtaining citizenship.
Humanitarian entrants and emergency assistance programs would still be accessible to non-citizens.
